bill_title: Sales and use tax; exemption for sale or lease of computer equipment of high-technology companies; revise spending threshold and extend sunset date
what_the_bill_did: Extended and broadened Georgia's sales-tax break for high-technology company computer equipment and high-technology data center equipment. The bill moved one sunset from June 30, 2023 to December 31, 2028, extended the separate data-center exemption into 2031, and set lower county-based thresholds that could be met with as few as 5 new quality jobs and $25 million in investment in smaller counties.
who_benefits: Data centers, cloud/computing operators, large server and storage facilities, and other qualifying high-technology companies.
estimated_public_cost: Available. A 2025 DOAA summary estimated $474.2 million in forgone state revenue in FY 2025 from the data-center sales-and-use-tax exemption program, plus local tax abatements on some projects.
political_campaign_takeaway: Strongest data-center hit. Anavitarte voted to extend a giant tax break that can be triggered in smaller counties with very few permanent jobs, while local communities can still be asked to absorb infrastructure and tax-base pressure.

bill_title: Sales and use tax; exemption for competitive projects of regional significance; change sunset provision
what_the_bill_did: Extended the sales-and-use-tax exemption on construction materials used in a competitive project of regional significance from June 30, 2023 to December 31, 2026.
who_benefits: Large business expansion or relocation projects that win state certification as a “competitive project of regional significance,” plus their contractors and suppliers.
estimated_public_cost: No clear bill-specific fiscal estimate found in public sources reviewed.
political_campaign_takeaway: Useful mega-project talking point: Anavitarte voted to keep a construction-materials tax break alive for hand-picked large projects rather than broad relief for ordinary taxpayers.

bill_title: Public utilities; one-time right of way permit fee and reduce annual right of way use fees; establish
what_the_bill_did: Created a one-time permit fee structure and reduced annual right-of-way use fees owed to municipal authorities by certain telephone companies that use public rights-of-way but do not serve retail end users inside the city.
who_benefits: Telephone, fiber, and communications infrastructure companies using city rights-of-way.
estimated_public_cost: No statewide public estimate found. The practical effect is a reduction in revenue flowing to municipalities from these companies.
political_campaign_takeaway: Strong local-taxpayer message. He voted to lower what certain telecom companies pay cities for using public rights-of-way, which can leave local governments to make up the difference elsewhere.

bill_title: Income tax; certain expenditures made by postproduction companies; extend tax credit
what_the_bill_did: Extended the state income tax credit for certain expenditures made by postproduction companies working on eligible productions in Georgia.
who_benefits: Postproduction houses, editing and sound facilities, and production companies using Georgia postproduction vendors.
estimated_public_cost: No separate bill-specific estimate found. For context, a 2023 DOAA-commissioned evaluation projected the broader Georgia film tax credit at $1.188 billion in FY 2026, and most of the film credit system has no overall annual cap.
political_campaign_takeaway: Good film-credit message if framed carefully: Anavitarte voted to prolong another slice of Georgia's huge entertainment incentive system, where the broader program costs the state more than $1 billion a year and many jobs are project-based rather than permanent.

bill_title: Income tax credit; film, gaming, video or digital production; revise a definition
what_the_bill_did: Revised definitions and administration for Georgia's film, gaming, video, or digital production credit, while preserving the broader transferable incentive structure.
who_benefits: Production companies and interactive entertainment firms seeking access to Georgia's entertainment tax-credit system.
estimated_public_cost: No bill-specific estimate found. The broader film tax credit system was projected at $1.188 billion in FY 2026 in a DOAA-commissioned evaluation.
political_campaign_takeaway: Not the cleanest stand-alone attack, but it reinforces a pattern: Anavitarte kept voting to maintain and fine-tune one of Georgia's largest business incentive systems.

bill_title: Income tax; rehabilitation of historic structures; revise tax credits
what_the_bill_did: Revised Georgia's historic rehabilitation tax-credit rules, including preapproval procedures and larger-value credits for certified structures. The enacted structure allowed credits of up to $5 million for many non-home projects, with higher-value treatment for some large job-creating projects.
who_benefits: Developers, commercial property owners, investors, and rehabilitation projects involving certified historic structures.
estimated_public_cost: Cap-based program; no bill-specific fiscal note located in public sources reviewed.
political_campaign_takeaway: Useful developer-incentive example, especially when tied to a broader pattern of targeted credits for favored projects rather than broad-based tax fairness.

bill_title: Revenue and taxation; rehabilitation of historic structures; revise tax credit
what_the_bill_did: Revised the historic rehabilitation credit so certain preapproved rehabilitations could claim a reduced credit in the taxable year when a certificate of occupancy is obtained.
who_benefits: Developers and owners of certified historic properties seeking more workable timing and access to the credit.
political_campaign_takeaway: Another example of Anavitarte backing targeted developer tax preferences, even if the bill is more technical than headline-grabbing.

bill_title: Revenue and taxation; medical equipment and supplies manufacturers and pharmaceutical and medicine manufacturers; provide tax credit
what_the_bill_did: Created a tax credit for qualifying medical equipment and supplies manufacturers and pharmaceutical and medicine manufacturers.
who_benefits: Selected manufacturers in the medical-equipment and pharmaceutical sectors.
estimated_public_cost: No clear bill-specific fiscal estimate found in public sources reviewed.
political_campaign_takeaway: Clean corporate-credit example: this was a direct tax subsidy for selected manufacturers, not general tax relief.

bill_title: Income tax; tax credits for establishing or relocating quality jobs; provide clarification
what_the_bill_did: Clarified eligibility rules for Georgia's quality jobs tax credit, ensuring that some otherwise tax-exempt entities could still benefit when they generate unrelated business income and establish or relocate qualifying jobs.
who_benefits: Employers using Georgia's quality jobs credit for expansions or relocations.
political_campaign_takeaway: A secondary example. More technical than a splashy giveaway, but still a vote to preserve and clarify a business tax credit aimed at selected employers.

bill_title: Income tax; expenditures on maintenance for Class III railroads; extend tax credit
what_the_bill_did: Extended the income tax credit for expenditures on maintenance of railroad track owned or leased by Class III railroads.
who_benefits: Short-line freight railroads and businesses tied to their service corridors.
estimated_public_cost: No clear bill-specific public estimate found.
political_campaign_takeaway: Good targeted-logistics example: another renewal of an industry-specific credit rather than broad relief for working families.

bill_title: Georgia Freight Railroad Program; eligible expenditures; procedures, conditions, and limitations for public and private financing of projects; provide
what_the_bill_did: Expanded eligible expenditures and financing tools for the Georgia Freight Railroad Program and added the transportation commissioner to the Georgia Ports Authority.
who_benefits: Freight rail carriers, related logistics infrastructure, and businesses tied to port and rail freight growth.
estimated_public_cost: No clear bill-specific public estimate found.
political_campaign_takeaway: This is a subsidy-style logistics incentive rather than a pure tax break, but it still reflects a vote to use state policy to support private freight and logistics interests.

bill_title: Transportation; eligible expenditures; Georgia Freight Railroad Program; provisions
what_the_bill_did: Expanded the Georgia Freight Railroad Program and added a state sales-and-use-tax exemption component tied to the measure.
who_benefits: Freight rail projects and related logistics development.
estimated_public_cost: No clear bill-specific public estimate found.
political_campaign_takeaway: A blended subsidy-and-tax-break example for freight/logistics interests.

bill_title: OneGeorgia Authority Act; grant program to support border region retail and tourism projects; provide
what_the_bill_did: Created a OneGeorgia grant program to support border-region retail and tourism projects.
who_benefits: Selected retail and tourism developments in counties near state borders.
estimated_public_cost: No clear bill-specific public estimate found.
political_campaign_takeaway: Direct subsidy example: state-backed aid for chosen retail and tourism projects.

bill_title: Ad valorem tax; timber equipment and timber products held by timber producers; provide exemption
what_the_bill_did: Created a statewide ad valorem tax exemption for timber equipment and timber products held by timber producers, subject to voter approval of the constitutional change.
who_benefits: Timber producers and firms holding qualifying timber equipment and timber inventory.
estimated_public_cost: No statewide estimate found. The bill reduces local property-tax bases where qualifying property would otherwise be taxable.
political_campaign_takeaway: Strong property-tax-contradiction message: another carveout that narrows the local tax base for a favored industry.

bill_title: Property Tax Exemptions; state-wide exemption; ad valorem taxes for aircraft used for the aerial application of fertilizers or other agricultural products; provide
what_the_bill_did: Created a statewide ad valorem tax exemption for aircraft used to apply fertilizer, pesticides, seeds, and other agricultural products, subject to voter approval of the constitutional change.
who_benefits: Crop-dusting and aerial agricultural application businesses.
estimated_public_cost: No statewide estimate found. The bill reduces local property-tax bases for qualifying aircraft.
political_campaign_takeaway: Niche business property-tax break that can be paired with a “who picks up the tab locally?” argument.

bill_title: Sales and use tax; exemption for sales of admissions to nonrecurring major sporting events; revise
what_the_bill_did: Extended the sales-tax exemption for admissions to nonrecurring major sporting events and broadened the definition to include FIFA World Cup matches.
who_benefits: Event promoters, venues, tourism boosters, and businesses tied to special-event traffic.
estimated_public_cost: No clear bill-specific public estimate found.
political_campaign_takeaway: Useful as a special-carveout example, though it is less potent than data centers or local property-tax breaks.

bill_title: Income tax; tax credits for contributions to rural hospital organizations; increase aggregate limit
what_the_bill_did: Increased the aggregate statewide cap for tax credits tied to contributions to rural hospital organizations.
who_benefits: Rural hospital organizations and donors claiming the credit.
estimated_public_cost: Mixed/public-interest credit. Later program materials show the statewide annual cap eventually reached $100 million; the public sources reviewed did not clearly isolate the exact fiscal effect of this 2022 increase alone.
political_campaign_takeaway: This is not a classic corporate giveaway. If used at all, it should be framed as a mixed credit that can help hospitals but still expands a targeted tax preference.
